sections.heading: What is an Intent?
sections.content: An Intent is a messaging object used to request an action from another component — within your own app or from another app entirely. Intents are the primary way Android components communicate with each other. You use them to start Activities, start Services, deliver Broadcasts, and pass data between screens. Think of an Intent as an envelope: it carries a message (what you want to do) and optionally a payload (data to pass along).

sections.heading: What Intents Can Do
items: Start a new Activity and navigate to another screen
Start a Service to perform background work
Deliver a Broadcast to multiple components listening for events
Pass data (extras) between Activities or components
Open another app's functionality (camera, browser, dialer, maps)
Return results back to the calling Activity using ActivityResultLauncher
Deep link into a specific screen of your app from a URL or notification

rows.explicit_intent: Directly specifies the target component by class name
rows.implicit_intent: Declares a general action; system finds a suitable component

rows.explicit_intent: Known — used within your own app
rows.implicit_intent: Unknown — resolved by the system at runtime
Open a URL, share text, pick an image, make a call

rows.explicit_intent: More secure — no ambiguity about the target
rows.implicit_intent: Less secure — another app could intercept it

sections.heading: Explicit Intents — Navigating Within Your App
sections.content: Explicit Intents are used when you know exactly which Activity or component you want to start. You create an Intent by passing the current Context and the target Activity class. You can then attach data using putExtra() before calling startActivity(). This is the standard way to move between screens within your own app.

sections.heading: Creating & Starting an Explicit Intent
items: val intent = Intent(this, TargetActivity::class.java) — create an explicit intent
intent.putExtra("key", value) — attach primitive data (String, Int, Boolean, etc.)
intent.putExtra("key", parcelableObject) — attach a Parcelable object
startActivity(intent) — starts the target Activity without expecting a result
The target Activity retrieves data using intent.getStringExtra("key") in onCreate()
Use intent.extras to get the full Bundle of all passed extras
Always provide safe defaults when reading extras (e.g., getStringExtra("key") ?: "default")

sections.heading: Implicit Intents — Opening System & Other Apps
sections.content: Implicit Intents do not name a specific component. Instead, you declare an action you want performed (like viewing a webpage or sharing text), and Android finds all apps that can handle it. If multiple apps can handle the intent, the user is shown a chooser dialog. If no app can handle it, your app will crash — so always check with resolveActivity() or use try-catch before firing an implicit intent.

rows.result: Opens the URL in the default browser
Opens the dialer with the number pre-filled
Opens the share sheet to share text
Share multiple files or images via share sheet
Opens email app with recipient pre-filled
Opens camera app to take a photo
Opens gallery for the user to pick an image

sections.heading: Getting a Result Back — ActivityResultLauncher
items: The modern way to get results from Activities is using the Activity Result API (replaces startActivityForResult)
Register a launcher using registerForActivityResult() in onCreate() or as a class property
Use ActivityResultContracts.StartActivityForResult() for generic result handling
Use built-in contracts like TakePicture, PickContact, RequestPermission for common tasks
Call launcher.launch(intent) to start the Activity
The result is delivered as an ActivityResult object containing resultCode and data Intent
Check result.resultCode == Activity.RESULT_OK before reading result data
The calling Activity uses setResult(RESULT_OK, intent) and finish() to send the result back

rows.use_case: General purpose result from any Activity
Request multiple runtime permissions at once
Capture a photo with the camera app
Let user pick any file from storage
Create and save a new document via SAF

sections.heading: Passing Data with Parcelable
sections.content: When passing complex objects between Activities via Intents, the object must implement Parcelable (recommended) or Serializable. Parcelable is Android's own serialization mechanism and is significantly faster than Java's Serializable. In Kotlin, you can use the @Parcelize annotation from the kotlin-parcelize plugin to automatically generate all required Parcelable implementation code.

sections.heading: Using @Parcelize for Easy Parcelable
items: Add plugin 'kotlin-parcelize' to your app-level build.gradle.kts plugins block
Annotate your data class with @Parcelize
Make the class implement Parcelable — the plugin generates the implementation automatically
Pass the object: intent.putExtra("user", userObject)
Retrieve it: val user = intent.getParcelableExtra<User>("user")
On API 33+, use intent.getParcelableExtra("user", User::class.java) for type safety
Parcelable works with nested objects as long as they are also Parcelable
Avoid passing very large objects via Intent — use a shared ViewModel or database ID instead

rows.parcelable: Fast — uses Android's optimized binary format

rows.serializable: None (but can be slow and error-prone)

sections.heading: Intent Flags
items: FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK – Starts the Activity in a new task (required when starting from non-Activity context)
FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP – If Activity already exists in stack, clears all Activities above it
FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP – Reuses existing instance if already at the top of the stack
FLAG_ACTIVITY_NO_HISTORY – The Activity is not added to the back stack (removed when user leaves)
FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK – Clears the entire existing task before starting the Activity
FLAG_ACTIVITY_REORDER_TO_FRONT – Brings an existing instance to the front instead of creating a new one
Combine flags using bitwise OR: intent.flags = FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK or FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK

rows.scenario: Logout and go to Login screen (clear all back stack)
Notification opens app to a specific screen
Splash screen — don't want it in back stack
Deep link into an existing Activity without recreating it
Start Activity from a Service or BroadcastReceiver

sections.heading: Handling Intents in the Target Activity
items: Access the incoming Intent using the intent property inside onCreate()
Use intent.getStringExtra("key") to read String extras
Use intent.action to check what action triggered this Activity
Use intent.data to get the URI passed with the Intent (e.g., for deep links)
Use intent.type to get the MIME type of shared content
Override onNewIntent(intent: Intent) to handle new Intents in singleTop or singleTask launch modes
Always null-check extras before using them — they may not always be present

sections.heading: Explicit vs Implicit Intent Decision Guide

rows.goal: Go to another screen in your app

rows.example: ACTION_SEND with text/plain or image/* type

sections.heading: Best Practices for Using Intents
items: Always use explicit Intents for navigation within your own app
Check if an implicit Intent can be resolved before calling startActivity() to avoid crashes
Use the Activity Result API (registerForActivityResult) instead of the deprecated startActivityForResult()
Prefer passing primitive IDs (e.g., userId: Int) over large objects — fetch the full object in the target
Use @Parcelize for any objects that must be passed between Activities
Define Intent extra keys as constants to avoid typos (e.g., companion object { const val EXTRA_USER_ID = "user_id" })
Use PendingIntent when starting components from Notifications, Widgets, or AlarmManager
Never pass sensitive data (passwords, tokens) through Intent extras — use secure storage instead

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Intents are the messaging backbone of Android, enabling communication between Activities, apps, and system components. Explicit Intents handle in-app navigation, while Implicit Intents open the door to the wider Android ecosystem. Mastering data passing with extras and Parcelable, using the Activity Result API for results, and applying the right Intent flags will give you full control over how your app navigates and communicates.
